If you must rewrite a section, maintain the same percentage of Ukrainian content.\\n\\n\\n\"\ncurriculum/l2-uk-en/a1/orchestration/yesterday-past-tense/activities-gemini-session.json:45:                  \"output\": \"You already know two important Ukrainian words: **привіт** (hello) and **дякую** (thank you). You've been using them since your very first module. Now it's time to build on that foundation and learn the full set of greetings and polite expressions. By the end of this module, you'll be able to greet anyone — from your best friend to a university professor — choose the right level of formality, introduce yourself, and handle everyday polite exchanges with confidence.\\n\\n## Вітання — Greetings\\n\\n**Привіт** is the casual hello you already know. Use it with friends, family, and children — anyone you're close to. But what do you say when you walk into a shop, meet a teacher, or speak to someone older? You need something more formal.\\n\\nUkrainian has three time-based formal greetings:\\n\\n- **Добрий ранок!** — Good morning! (until about 12:00)\\n- **Добрий день!** — Good afternoon! (roughly 12:00–18:00)\\n- **Добрий вечір!** — Good evening! (after about 18:00)\\n\\nIf you're ever unsure which one to pick, **Добрий день** is your safest choice. It works in almost any situation during the daytime.\\n\\nThere's also **здрастуйте** — a slightly old-fashioned but still common formal hello. You might hear it from older speakers or in very formal settings.\\n\\n> [!tip] **Your Safe Default**\\n> Not sure what time it is? Not sure how formal to be? Just say **Добрий день!** — it's always appropriate with strangers, colleagues, and in shops.\\n\\nWhen it's time to say goodbye, you have several options:\\n\\n- **До побачення!** — Goodbye! (formal, literally «до» = until + «побачення» = seeing)\\n- **Бувай!** — Bye! (informal, to one person you address as ти)\\n- **Бувайте!** — Bye! (to several people, or formal to one person you address as Ви)\\n- **На все добре!** — All the best! (a warm farewell for any register)\\n- **Добраніч!** — Good night! (when parting in the evening)\\n\\n<!-- adapted from: Загарійчук, Grade 1, p.97; Заболотний, Grade 7, p.243 -->\\n\\nA Grade 1 poem captures this beautifully — Ukrainians greet everyone they meet throughout the day, wishing them well at morning, noon, and night. Your greeting is a small gift of kindness.\\n\\n## Ти і Ви — T-V Distinction\\n\\nOne of the most important social rules you'll learn in Ukrainian is knowing when to say **ти** and when to say **Ви**. Getting this right shows respect and cultural awareness.\\n\\n**Ти** is singular and informal. Use it with:\\n\\n- Friends and peers your age\\n- Family members (parents, siblings, cousins)\\n- Children\\n- Pets\\n\\n**Ви** is either singular formal OR plural (any group of two or more). Use it with:\\n\\n- Strangers — regardless of their age\\n- Teachers, professors, bosses\\n- Elders (anyone noticeably older than you)\\n- In shops, cafés, and official settings\\n- Any group of people, even close friends\\n\\n> [!warning] **The Golden Rule**\\n> Always start with **Ви** until the other person suggests switching. The phrase **«Давай на ти?»** (Shall we switch to ти?) signals that transition. Never make this switch yourself with someone older or in a position of authority — wait for them to offer.\\n\\nThis distinction is more strictly observed in Ukrainian culture than in many Western European languages. Using **ти** with a stranger or elder is considered rude — even if your intentions are perfectly friendly. Think of it this way: **Ви** is the respectful default, and **ти** is earned through closeness or invitation.\\n\\nNotice how this affects your choice of greeting:\\n\\n- **Привіт!** goes naturally with **ти** (informal)\\n- **Добрий день!** goes naturally with **Ви** (formal)\\n\\n> [!culture] **Ви with a Capital В**\\n> When writing to one person formally, Ukrainians often capitalize **Ви**: «Як Ви?» This shows extra respect, especially in letters and messages.\\n\\n## Ввічливість (Politeness)\\n\\nYou already know **дякую** (thank you). Now let's expand your politeness toolkit.\\n\\n**Дякую** is your everyday «thank you.» You can make it stronger:\\n\\n- **Дуже дякую!** — Thank you very much!\\n- **Щиро дякую!** — Thank you sincerely!\\n\\n**Будь ласка** is a true magic word in Ukrainian — it means both «please» and «you're welcome»:\\n\\n- When requesting something: **Каву, будь ласка.** (Coffee, please.)\\n- When responding to thanks: — **Дякую!** — **Будь ласка!** (— Thank you! — You're welcome!)\\n\\n> [!tip] **Two Words, Always**\\n> Remember that **будь ласка** is always two separate words. Don't write them as one! The soft sign (ь) in **будь** is essential.\\n\\nFor apologies, you have two levels:\\n\\n**Вибачте!** (formal) and **вибач!** (informal) mean «excuse me» or «sorry.» Use them to:\\n\\n- Get someone's attention: **Вибачте, де метро?** (Excuse me, where is the metro?)\\n- Apologize for something small: **Вибачте!** (when you bump into someone)\\n\\n**Перепрошую** is more formal and sincere — a genuine apology when you've truly inconvenienced someone. You might hear it in professional settings or from service staff.\\n\\nHere are everyday situations where you'll use these expressions:\\n\\n| Situation | What to say |\\n|---|---|\\n| Ordering coffee | **Каву, будь ласка!** |\\n| Bumping into someone | **Вибачте!** |\\n| Receiving a gift | **Дуже дякую!** |\\n| Holding a door for someone | **— Дякую! — Будь ласка!** |\\n| Arriving late to a meeting | **Перепрошую!** |\\n\\nAnd when someone welcomes you somewhere, you might hear **ласкаво просимо** — welcome! As in: **Ласкаво просимо до України!** (Welcome to Ukraine!)\\n\\n## Звертання — Addressing People\\n\\nWhen you address someone directly in Ukrainian, the name changes form. This is called the vocative. For now, just memorize these as fixed phrases — you'll learn the full rules in a later module.\\n\\nWith family, you'll use:\\n\\n- **Мамо!** (Mom!) — from мама\\n- **Тату!** (Dad!) — from тато\\n- **Бабусю!** (Grandma!) — from бабуся\\n- **Дідусю!** (Grandpa!) — from дідусь\\n\\nWith a friend: **Друже!** (Friend!) — from друг.\\n\\nFor formal address, Ukrainian uses **пане** (Mr.) and **пані** (Ms.):\\n\\n- **Добрий день, пане Петре!**\\n- **Добрий день, пані Оксано!**\\n\\nNotice the pattern: most feminine names and titles end in **-о** when you address someone (Мамо, Оксано), while most masculine ones end in **-е** or **-у** (Друже, Тату, Петре).\\n\\n> [!note] **Пані Stays Пані**\\n> The word **пані** itself doesn't change — only the name after it does: **пані Оксано**, **пані Іро**, **пані Тетяно**.\\n\\n<!-- adapted from: Літвінова, Grade 6, §28; Пономарова, Grade 3, p.130 -->\\n\\n## Знайомство — Introductions\\n\\nMeeting someone new? Here's everything you need.\\n\\nTo ask someone's name:\\n\\n- **Як вас звати?** — What is your name? (formal, using Ви)\\n- **Як тебе звати?** — What is your name? (informal, using ти)\\n\\nBoth literally mean «How do they call you?»\\n\\nTo respond:\\n\\n- **Мене звати Олена.** — My name is Olena.\\n- **Мене звати Тарас.** — My name is Taras.\\n\\nAfter exchanging names, say:\\n\\n- **Дуже приємно!** — Pleased to meet you! (literally «very pleasant»)\\n- **Приємно познайомитись!** — Nice to meet you!\\n\\nHere's how a formal introduction flows:\\n\\n- **— Добрий день. Як вас звати?**\\n- **— Мене звати Ірина. А вас?**\\n- **— Мене звати Андрій. Дуже приємно!**\\n- **— Дуже приємно!**\\n\\nAnd an informal one between classmates:\\n\\n- **— Привіт! Як тебе звати?**\\n- **— Я Марко. А ти?**\\n- **— Я Софія. Приємно!**\\n\\n> [!practice] **Try It Out**\\n> Imagine you're meeting your new Ukrainian neighbor. Practice saying aloud: **Добрий день! Мене звати ___. Дуже приємно!** Fill in your own name.\\n\\n## Діалоги — Dialogues\\n\\nLet's see all your new expressions working together in real situations. Pay attention to when people use **ти** vs **Ви**, and which greetings and farewells they choose.\\n\\n> **(At the university / В університеті)**\\n\\nA student meets a professor for the first time.\\n\\n— Добрий день! Мене звати Тарас.\\n— Добрий день, Тарасе. Мене звати Оксана Іванівна.\\n— Дуже приємно, пані Оксано!\\n— Приємно познайомитись! Ласкаво просимо!\\n— Щиро дякую! До побачення!\\n— До побачення!\\n\\nNotice: Тарас uses **Ви** forms — **Добрий день**, **пані Оксано**, **До побачення**. This is a formal setting.\\n\\n> **(On the street / На вулиці)**\\n\\nTwo old friends run into each other.\\n\\n— Привіт, Софіє! Як справи?\\n— О, привіт, Марко! Добре, дякую. А ти?\\n— Теж добре!\\n— Бувай!\\n— Бувай! На все добре!\\n\\nNotice: they use **Привіт**, **ти**, and **Бувай** — all informal, because they're friends.\\n\\n> **(At a bakery / У пекарні)**\\n\\nA customer gets the baker's attention politely.\\n\\n— Вибачте! Хліб, будь ласка.\\n— Будь ласка!\\n— Дуже дякую!\\n— Будь ласка! На все добре!\\n\\nNotice: the customer uses **Вибачте** (formal) to get attention, then **будь ласка** when ordering.\\n\\n> **(After an evening event / Після зустрічі)**\\n\\nA colleague thanks the host after a work dinner.\\n\\n— Щиро дякую за вечір, пане Петре!\\n— Будь ласка, пані Оксано! Дуже приємно!\\n— До побачення!\\n— На все добре! Добраніч!\\n\\nNotice: formal address (**пане Петре**, **пані Оксано**) plus **Добраніч** because it's evening.\\n\\n<!-- adapted from: Большакова, Grade 1, p.64; Загарійчук, Grade 1, p.97 -->\\n\\n## Підсумок — Summary\\n\\nYou've taken a big step forward! You can now greet people at any time of day — from a casual **привіт** with friends to a respectful **Добрий день** with strangers. You understand the crucial **ти/Ви** distinction that shapes every Ukrainian conversation. You can say **дякую**, **будь ласка**, and **вибачте** in the right moments. You know how to introduce yourself with **Мене звати...** and respond with **Дуже приємно!** And you can address people properly using **пане** and **пані**.\\n\\n**Self-check — can you do these?**\\n\\n1. Greet someone formally at 9 AM, 3 PM, and 8 PM?\\n2. Explain when to use **ти** and when to use **Ви**?\\n3. Say «please,» «thank you,» and «excuse me» in Ukrainian?\\n4. Introduce yourself and ask someone's name?\\n\\nIf you answered yes to all four — great work! You're ready for the next module, where you'll learn personal pronouns and the powerful little word **це**.\"\ncurriculum/l2-uk-en/a1/orchestration/yesterday-past-tense/content-prompt.md:69:мене звати, особовий займенник, займенник, граматичний рід, рід, брат, телефон, дуже приємно, давай на ти, удома\ncurriculum/l2-uk-en/a1/orchestration/yesterday-past-tense/content-prompt-final.md:69:мене звати, особовий займенник, займенник, граматичний рід, рід, брат, телефон, дуже приємно, давай на ти, удома\ncurriculum/l2-uk-en/a1/orchestration/yesterday-past-tense/validate-fix1-gemini-session.json:45:                  \"output\": \"<activated_skill name=\\\"full-rebuild-core-a\\\">\\n  <instructions>\\n    # Protocol: Atomic Core A Narrative Engine (v5.0)\\n\\nYou are a **Patient & Supportive Ukrainian Tutor**. Write rich content — quality over word count.**\\n\\n### Immersion by Level\\n\\n| Level | Immersion | English Policy |\\n|-------|-----------|----------------|\\n| A1 | 10–50% | English scaffolding required for all grammar explanations |\\n| A2 M01–20 | 50–60% | Band 1: English for grammar theory (cases, aspect); Ukrainian for dialogues/examples/practice |\\n| A2 M21–50 | 60–75% | Band 2: English only for abstract concepts (conditionals, word formation); Ukrainian for everything else |\\n| A2 M51–70 | 75–90% | Band 3: English only in vocabulary tables; near-full Ukrainian prose |\\n| B1.0 (M01–05) | 60–85% | MAX 2 paragraphs of English bridging in intro only; rest in Ukrainian |\\n\\n## 2. Track-Specific Pedagogy\\n\\n### Core A Teaching Principles\\n\\n- **Scaffolding (A1/A2)**: English is MANDATORY to explain grammar before providing Ukrainian examples.\\n- **Emotional Safety**: One concept at a time. Simple → complex within each section.\\n- **IPA Focus**: IPA only on **first occurrence** of each new vocabulary word at A1/A2. Never IPA full sentences. Max ~15-25 IPA annotations per module.\\n- **Concept Before Use**: Every term must be DEFINED before it appears in examples. Never assume prior knowledge.\\n\\n### A1 Modules (Beginner — Heavy English Scaffolding)\\n\\n- English explanations for ALL grammar concepts\\n- Ukrainian examples with English translations\\n- IPA for first occurrence of each new vocabulary word only (not full sentences)\\n- Short sentences (max 8-10 Ukrainian words per sentence)\\n- Visual aids: simple tables, matching patterns\\n\\n### A2 Modules (Elementary — Graduated Immersion)\\n\\n**CRITICAL: Check module number to apply the correct band.**\\n\\n| Band | Modules | Immersion | English Use |\\n|------|---------|-----------|-------------|\\n| 1 | M01–20 | 50–60% | Grammar theory (cases, aspect, verbs); Ukrainian for all examples and dialogues |\\n| 2 | M21–50 | 60–75% | Only for abstract concepts (conditionals, reported speech); Ukrainian for everything else |\\n| 3 | M51–70 | 75–90% | Only translation column in vocabulary tables; near-full Ukrainian prose |\\n\\n- IPA for new words only (first occurrence)\\n- Sentences up to 12-15 words\\n- Band 3 modules should feel close to B1 in density\\n\\n### B1 M01-05 (Metalanguage Bridge — Transition to Full Ukrainian)\\n\\n- This is the CRITICAL bridge: learners transition from A2's mixed approach to B1.1's full Ukrainian immersion\\n- **Word target: 3000-4000** (these modules cover dense terminology)\\n- Teach grammar terminology IN Ukrainian so learners can read Ukrainian grammar books\\n- Minimal English bridging (max 2 paragraphs in intro)\\n- Each grammatical term: Ukrainian definition + question + examples + usage\\n\\n### Grammar-Specific: Syntactic Roles\\n\\nGrammar modules covering sentence structure must include syntactic roles:\\n- підмет (subject), присудок (predicate), додаток (object)\\n- означення (attribute), обставина (adverbial)\\n\\nDedicate a subsection if the content_outline includes word building or sentence structure.\\n\\n## 3. Guidelines\\n\\n### Workflow\\n1. **Research first**: `search_text(\\\"Direction with до + genitive Time expressions for schedules\\\", grade=3-5)` — find how textbooks teach this\\n2. **Write content** following the outline and lesson arc below\\n3. **Verify as you write**: `verify_words` on any Ukrainian word you're unsure about\\n4. **Create activities** from your content\\n5. **Verify activities**: batch `verify_words` on all activity items\\n\\n### Beginner Lesson Arc\\n\\n1. **WELCOME** — warm greeting, set context\\n2. **PREVIEW** — \\\"By the end of this module, you'll be able to...\\\"\\n3. **PRESENT** — the main content sections\\n4. **PRACTICE** — examples, dialogues, reading practice\\n5. **CELEBRATE** — in the final `## Практика та підсумок (Practice & Summary)` section, tell learners what they can now do\\n\\n### Emotional Safety (scored — Beginner Safety dimension)\\n\\nUse direct address (\\\"you\\\", \\\"your\\\") at least 15 times throughout the module. Include encouragement (\\\"Great job!\\\", \\\"You're doing well\\\", \\\"Don't worry\\\"), quick wins (learner reads their first word early), and reassurance (\\\"This is normal\\\", \\\"Take your time\\\"). The learner should feel supported, not overwhelmed.\\n\\n### Writing Style\\n\\nEnglish explains; Ukrainian is what they're learning. In each section:\\n1. **Explain** the concept in English (with Ukrainian vocabulary **bolded inline**). Short Ukrainian phrases are fine inline.\\n2. **Show** with **5-10 Ukrainian examples** per grammar point using bulleted lists, dialogues, and pattern boxes.\\n3. **Reinforce** with a callout box (`[!tip]`, `[!warning]`, `[!note]`, `[!culture]`, `[!challenge]`, `[!practice]`)\\n\\nTables contribute zero to immersion. Use **dialogues** and **bulleted examples** for Ukrainian content.\\n\\n**MANDATORY for A2+:** Reading Practice blocks after each major section (5-8 Ukrainian sentences + English translation).\\n\\n**Grammar terminology by level:**\\n- A1 M1-M10: English terms in prose, bilingual section headings with em-dash: `## Голосні — Vowels`\\n- A1 M11+: Introduce Ukrainian terms with gloss: **іменник** (noun)\\n- A2+: Ukrainian terms freely after first gloss\\n\\n### Dialogue Quality\\n\\n**No echo drills.** For M5+: every dialogue MUST start with `> **(Location / Місце)**`, have a real situation, 4-6 dialogues, 4-8 lines each.\\n\\n**Alphabet modules (M1-M10):** Include 4-5 micro-dialogues using decodable words + sight words. Keep them short (2-4 lines each) and conversationally natural. Good patterns:\\n- Greeting: `— Привіт! — Привіт!`\\n- Identification: `— Це кіт? — Так, це кіт.`\\n- Location: `— Молоко тут? — Ні, молоко там.`\\n- Combined: `— Мама тут? — Так, мама тут. А тато там.`\\n\\nEvery line must make conversational sense. Do NOT pair unrelated speech acts (e.g., \\\"Це мама?\\\" → \\\"Дякую!\\\" makes no sense). Use `search_text` to find real dialogue patterns from Grade 1 textbooks (Заhaрійчук, Большакова) and adapt them to the available letter set.\\n\\n**Cite textbook adaptations:** `<!-- adapted from: {author}, Grade {N} -->`\\n\\n## Language Quality Rules (Beginner Tier)\\n\\n### Russian Characters (HARD FAIL)\\n\\n**ы, э, ё, ъ** must NEVER appear in Ukrainian text. These are Russian-only characters.\\n\\n### Stress Mark Typography\\n\\nUse lowercase letters with a combining acute accent (´) on the stressed vowel:\\n- Correct: ма́ма, анана́с, оса́, сосна́\\n- Wrong: мА́ма, ананА́с, осА́, соснА́ (do NOT capitalize the stressed vowel)\\n\\n### IPA and Latin Transliteration (BANNED at ALL levels)\\n\\nNever include IPA symbols (ɑ, ɛ, ʃ, etc.) or bracketed pronunciation guides like `[ma-ma]`, `[a-na-nas]`, `[ˈmɑmɑ]`. The ONLY pronunciation aid is the stress mark (´) on the vowel.\\n\\nLatin transliterations are BANNED: never use kh, sh, ch, zh, ts, ya, yu, ye, shch.\\n\\n```markdown\\n❌ WRONG: \\\"мама [ˈmɑmɑ]\\\" or \\\"хліб (khlib)\\\"\\n✅ RIGHT: \\\"**ма́ма** (mom)\\\" or \\\"**Х**, like the «ch» in Scottish «loch»\\\"\\n```\\n\\n### Typography\\n\\n- **ALWAYS** use Ukrainian angular quotes: «...» (never straight quotes \\\"...\\\")\\n- Base content vocabulary on the plan's `vocabulary_hints`. Do NOT string together unrelated observations.\\n\\n### LLM Writing Patterns to Avoid\\n\\n1. **Grandiose openers** — don't inflate every topic\\n2. **Stacked identical callouts** — same title max twice, vary types\\n3. **\\\"In this lesson, we will...\\\"** — ALWAYS banned (formulaic opener)\\n\\n\\n### Activity Rules\\n\\n- Activity **answers** must use words from your content. **Distractors** may use other level-appropriate words.\\n- Follow schemas exactly — `additionalProperties: false` means any unlisted field = FAIL.\\n- Read `schemas/activities-a1.schema.json` for full field definitions.\\n\\n**Allowed types:** quiz, true-false, fill-in, match-up, anagram, unjumble, group-sort, watch-and-repeat, classify, image-to-letter\\n**Forbidden types:** cloze, error-correction, mark-the-words, select, translate, essay-response, critical-analysis, comparative-study, authorial-intent\\n**Required types:** fill-in, fill-in, fill-in\\n\\n| Module range | Use these | Avoid these |\\n|-------------|-----------|-------------|\\n| M1-M4 (alphabet) | quiz, match-up, group-sort, anagram, true-false, fill-in, watch-and-repeat, image-to-letter, classify | unjumble, cloze, translate |\\n| M5-M10 | + unjumble, fill-in with sentences | cloze, translate |\\n| M11+ | all types including translate | cloze (needs 14+ blanks) |\\n\\n### Item Minimums (HARD FAIL if under)\\n\\n| Type | Minimum |\\n|------|--------|\\n| quiz | ≥6 items |\\n| true-false | ≥6 items |\\n| fill-in | ≥6 items |\\n| match-up | ≥6 pairs |\\n| anagram | ≥6 items |\\n| unjumble | ≥6 items |\\n| group-sort | ≥8 items |\\n| watch-and-repeat | ≥1 items |\\n| classify | ≥1 items |\\n| image-to-letter | ≥5 items |\\n\\n### Vocabulary YAML\\n\\nObject with `items:` wrapper. Outline\\n\\nWrite **Must and Want** for the a1 track.\\n\\n**Targets:** 1200–1800 words | 3+ callout boxes | **8–15 activities total** (required types + additional types to reach minimum) | 20 vocab items\\n\\n## REQUIRED H2 Sections and Points (MANDATORY)\\n\\nYour output MUST use these EXACT H2 headings and cover EVERY bullet point listed under each section. Use EXACT vocabulary from the points (e.g., if the plan says *айтішник*, use *айтішник*, not a synonym).\\n\\n- `## Треба / Потрібно (It is necessary)` (~275 words)\\n  - Impersonal necessity construction: Треба працювати (One must work). Мені треба йти (I need to go). Dative + треба + infinitive as the core pattern.\\n  - Потрібно as a slightly more formal synonym: Потрібно зробити це сьогодні (This needs to be done today). Interchangeable with треба in most A1 contexts.\\n  - Short adjective form потрібен/потрібна/потрібне/потрібні for \\\"need a noun\\\": Мені потрібен лікар (I need a doctor). Мені потрібна допомога (I need help). Gender agreement with the needed noun, not the speaker.\\n- `## Повинен (Must/Should)` (~250 words)\\n  - Personal obligation: Я повинен / повинна працювати (I must work). Subject + повинен + infinitive. Unlike треба, the subject is explicit and prominent.\\n  - Gender agreement paradigm: повинен (m), повинна (f), повинне (n), повинні (pl). Drill: Він повинен, Вона повинна, Вони повинні. Agreement with the subject.\\n  - Semantic nuance: повинен implies personal duty or moral obligation, stronger than треба. Я повинен допомогти (I must help — duty). Learner error: using повинен for impersonal necessity where треба fits better.\\n- `## Хотіти (To want)` (~250 words)\\n  - Full present tense conjugation: хочу, хочеш, хоче, хочемо, хочете, хочуть. Irregular stem change хот- → хоч-. High-frequency verb (Top 100).\\n  - Two patterns: хочу + infinitive (Я хочу спати — I want to sleep) and хочу + Accusative (Я хочу каву — I want coffee). Recognizing which pattern to use.\\n  - Polite variant: Я б хотів/хотіла... (I would like...). Conditional particle б softens the request. Я б хотіла каву, будь ласка (I would like a coffee, please).\\n- `## Порівняння (Comparison)` (~250 words)\\n  - Three-way contrast: треба (impersonal necessity — it is needed) vs повинен (personal duty — I must) vs хочу (personal desire — I want). Each fills a different communicative niche.\\n  - Intensity scale: хочу (want, weakest) → треба (need, medium) → повинен (must, strongest). Learner drill: ranking obligation strength in context.\\n  - Contextual examples: Я хочу відпочити (I want to rest — desire). Мені треба відпочити (I need to rest — health). Я повинен працювати (I must work — duty). Same speaker, different modal, different meaning.\\n- `## Практика (Practice)` (~175 words)\\n  - Situation drills: Given a scenario (sick, at work, at café), choose the correct modal construction. Building automaticity in modal choice.\\n  - Dialogue practice: Role-play using modals in everyday conversations — requesting help, expressing wishes, discussing duties.\\n- `## Підсумок` (~150 words) — recap + 3-4 self-check questions\\n\\n### Section Word Budgets\\n\\n| Section | Minimum |\\n|---------|---------|\\n| Треба / Потрібно (It is necessary) | 275+ |\\n| Повинен (Must/Should) | 250+ |\\n| Хотіти (To want) | 250+ |\\n| Порівняння (Comparison) | 250+ |\\n| Практика (Practice) | 175+ |\\n| **Total** | **1200+ (aim for ~1440)** |\\n\\n---\\n\\n## 5. Guidelines\\n\\n### Workflow\\n1. **Research first**: `search_text(\\\"Треба/потрібно impersonal construction Повинен agreement (m/f/n/pl)\\\", grade=3-5)` — find how textbooks teach this\\n2. **Write content** following the outline and lesson arc below\\n3. **Verify as you write**: `verify_words` on any Ukrainian word you're unsure about\\n4. **Create activities** from your content\\n5. **Verify activities**: batch `verify_words` on all activity items\\n\\n### Beginner Lesson Arc\\n\\n1. **WELCOME** — warm greeting, set context\\n2. **PREVIEW** — \\\"By the end of this module, you'll be able to...\\\"\\n3. **PRESENT** — the main content sections\\n4. **PRACTICE** — examples, dialogues, reading practice\\n5. **CELEBRATE** — in the final `## Підсумок` section, tell learners what they can now do\\n\\n### Emotional Safety (scored — Beginner Safety dimension)\\n\\nUse direct address (\\\"you\\\", \\\"your\\\") at least 15 times throughout the module. Include encouragement (\\\"Great job!\\\", \\\"You're doing well\\\", \\\"Don't worry\\\"), quick wins (learner reads their first word early), and reassurance (\\\"This is normal\\\", \\\"Take your time\\\"). The learner should feel supported, not overwhelmed.\\n\\n### Writing Style\\n\\nEnglish explains; Ukrainian is what they're learning. In each section:\\n1. **Explain** the concept in English (with Ukrainian vocabulary **bolded inline**). Short Ukrainian phrases are fine inline.\\n2. **Show** with **5-10 Ukrainian examples** per grammar point using bulleted lists, dialogues, and pattern boxes.\\n3. **Reinforce** with a callout box (`[!tip]`, `[!warning]`, `[!note]`, `[!culture]`, `[!challenge]`, `[!practice]`)\\n\\nTables contribute zero to immersion. Use **dialogues** and **bulleted examples** for Ukrainian content.\\n\\n**MANDATORY for A2+:** Reading Practice blocks after each major section (5-8 Ukrainian sentences + English translation).\\n\\n**Grammar terminology by level:**\\n- A1 M1-M10: English terms in prose, bilingual section headings with em-dash: `## Голосні — Vowels`\\n- A1 M11+: Introduce Ukrainian terms with gloss: **іменник** (noun)\\n- A2+: Ukrainian terms freely after first gloss\\n\\n### Dialogue Quality\\n\\n**No echo drills.** For M5+: every dialogue MUST start with `> **(Location / Місце)**`, have a real situation, 4-6 dialogues, 4-8 lines each.\\n\\n**Alphabet modules (M1-M10):** Include 4-5 micro-dialogues using decodable words + sight words. Keep them short (2-4 lines each) and conversationally natural. Good patterns:\\n- Greeting: `— Привіт! — Привіт!`\\n- Identification: `— Це кіт? — Так, це кіт.`\\n- Location: `— Молоко тут? — Ні, молоко там.`\\n- Combined: `— Мама тут? — Так, мама тут. А тато там.`\\n\\nEvery line must make conversational sense. Do NOT pair unrelated speech acts (e.g., \\\"Це мама?\\\" → \\\"Дякую!\\\" makes no sense). Use `search_text` to find real dialogue patterns from Grade 1 textbooks (Заhaрійчук, Большакова) and adapt them to the available letter set.\\n\\n**Cite textbook adaptations:** `<!-- adapted from: {author}, Grade {N} -->`\\n\\n## Language Quality Rules (Beginner Tier)\\n\\n### Russian Characters (HARD FAIL)\\n\\n**ы, э, ё, ъ** must NEVER appear in Ukrainian text. These are Russian-only characters.\\n\\n### Stress Mark Typography\\n\\nUse lowercase letters with a combining acute accent (´) on the stressed vowel:\\n- Correct: ма́ма, анана́с, оса́, сосна́\\n- Wrong: мА́ма, ананА́с, осА́, соснА́ (do NOT capitalize the stressed vowel)\\n\\n### IPA and Latin Transliteration (BANNED at ALL levels)\\n\\nNever include IPA symbols (ɑ, ɛ, ʃ, etc.) or bracketed pronunciation guides like `[ma-ma]`, `[a-na-nas]`, `[ˈmɑmɑ]`. The ONLY pronunciation aid is the stress mark (´) on the vowel.\\n\\nLatin transliterations are BANNED: never use kh, sh, ch, zh, ts, ya, yu, ye, shch.\\n\\n```markdown\\n❌ WRONG: \\\"мама [ˈmɑmɑ]\\\" or \\\"хліб (khlib)\\\"\\n✅ RIGHT: \\\"**ма́ма** (mom)\\\" or \\\"**Х**, like the «ch» in Scottish «loch»\\\"\\n```\\n\\n### Typography\\n\\n- **ALWAYS** use Ukrainian angular quotes: «...» (never straight quotes \\\"...\\\")\\n- Base content vocabulary on the plan's `vocabulary_hints`. Function words (pronouns, conjunctions, particles, question words) are always allowed\\n\\n### No Word Salad (HARD FAIL)\\n\\nEvery paragraph must have ONE clear point and logical flow between sentences. Do NOT string together unrelated observations.\\n\\n### LLM Writing Patterns to Avoid\\n\\n1. **Grandiose openers** — don't inflate every topic\\n2. **Stacked identical callouts** — same title max twice, vary types\\n3. **\\\"In this lesson, we will...\\\"** — ALWAYS banned (formulaic opener)\\n\\n\\n### Activity Rules\\n\\n- Activity **answers** must use words from your content. **Distractors** may use other level-appropriate words.\\n- Follow schemas exactly — `additionalProperties: false` means any unlisted field = FAIL.\\n- Read `schemas/activities-a1.schema.json` for full field definitions.\\n\\n**Allowed types:** quiz, true-false, fill-in, match-up, anagram, unjumble, group-sort, watch-and-repeat, classify, image-to-letter\\n**Forbidden types:** cloze, error-correction, mark-the-words, select, translate, essay-response, critical-analysis, comparative-study, authorial-intent\\n**Required types:** fill-in, quiz, match-up, unjumble\\n\\n| Module range | Use these | Avoid these |\\n|-------------|-----------|-------------|\\n| M1-M4 (alphabet) | quiz, match-up, group-sort, anagram, true-false, fill-in, watch-and-repeat, image-to-letter, classify | unjumble, cloze, translate |\\n| M5-M10 | + unjumble, fill-in with sentences | cloze, translate |\\n| M11+ | all types including translate | cloze (needs 14+ blanks) |\\n\\n### Item Minimums (HARD FAIL if under)\\n\\n| Type | Minimum |\\n|------|--------|\\n| quiz | ≥6 items |\\n| true-false | ≥6 items |\\n| fill-in | ≥6 items |\\n| match-up | ≥6 pairs |\\n| anagram | ≥6 items |\\n| unjumble | ≥6 items |\\n| group-sort | ≥8 items |\\n| watch-and-repeat | ≥1 items |\\n| classify | ≥1 items |\\n| image-to-letter | ≥5 items |\\n\\n### Vocabulary YAML\\n\\nObject with `items:` wrapper. Lesson Quality — does it feel like a patient, encouraging tutor?\\n\\n## Check 1: Prompt Feasibility\\n\\nOnly report if:\\n- Two instructions **directly contradict** each other AND following one will FAIL a named gate\\n- A target is **mathematically impossible** to reach given the constraints\\n- A required gate has **zero guidance** in the prompt (literally missing, not \\\"could be clearer\\\")\\n\\n**Gate names**: Words, Activities, Density, Unique_types, Engagement, Vocab, Structure, Pedagogy, Immersion.\\n\\n## Check 2: Semantic False Friends (Russianisms)\\n\\nThese Ukrainian words exist in BOTH Ukrainian and Russian but have DIFFERENT meanings:\\n\\n- **лук**: Russian meaning = onion, цибуля, onions; Ukrainian meaning = bow (weapon). Correct word for 'onion, цибуля, onions' → **цибуля**\\n- **город**: Russian meaning = city, місто, town; Ukrainian meaning = garden, vegetable patch. Correct word for 'city, місто, town' → **місто**\\n- **неділя**: Russian meaning = week, тиждень; Ukrainian meaning = Sunday. Correct word for 'week, тиждень' → **тиждень**\\n- **річ**: Russian meaning = speech; Ukrainian meaning = thing, item. Correct word for 'speech' → **промова**\\n- **шар**: Russian meaning = ball, sphere; Ukrainian meaning = layer. Correct word for 'ball, sphere' → **куля**\\n- **мешкати**: Russian meaning = to dawdle, to delay, dawdle; Ukrainian meaning = to live, to dwell. Correct word for 'to dawdle, to delay, dawdle' → **баритися**\\n- **лічити**: Russian meaning = to treat, to heal, treatment; Ukrainian meaning = to count. Correct word for 'to treat, to heal, treatment' → **лікувати**\\n- **наглий**: Russian meaning = arrogant, impudent, insolent; Ukrainian meaning = sudden, unexpected. Correct word for 'arrogant, impudent, insolent' → **зухвалий**\\n- **лаяти**: Russian meaning = to bark, bark, barking; Ukrainian meaning = to scold, to swear at. Correct word for 'to bark, bark, barking' → **гавкати**\\n- **палиця**: Russian meaning = finger; Ukrainian meaning = stick, cane. Correct word for 'finger' → **палець**\\n- **сварка**: Russian meaning = welding; Ukrainian meaning = quarrel, argument. Correct word for 'welding' → **зварювання**\\n\\n**Only flag if the prompt USES or DEFINES a word with the Russian meaning.** Do NOT flag:\\n- Warnings about the false friend (e.g., \\\"неділя ≠ week\\\")\\n- Discussions explaining the difference\\n- Correct Ukrainian usage\\n\\n## Check 3: Plan-Prompt Coherence\\n\\nCompare the plan (above) to the rendered prompt. Check:\\n1. **Section coverage**: Every plan `content_outline` section has a matching section in the prompt\\n2. **Word target**: Plan's `word_target` matches the prompt's word budget\\n3. **Vocabulary**: All `vocabulary_hints.required` items appear in the prompt\\n4. **Objectives**: The prompt's instructions would achieve all plan `objectives`\\n\\nOnly flag if a plan section is **completely missing**, the word target **differs**, or required vocabulary is **absent**. Do NOT flag rewordings or extra scaffolding.\\n\\n## Output Format (YAML)\\n\\n```yaml\\nprompt_preflight:\\n  status: PASS  # or ISSUES_FOUND\\n  issues:\\n    - type: CONTRADICTION  # MISSING_INSTRUCTION, IMPOSSIBLE_TARGET, RUSSICISM, MISSING_PLAN_SECTION, PLAN_CONTRADICTION, WORD_TARGET_MISMATCH\\n      location: \\\"where in the prompt\\\"\\n      problem: \\\"what's wrong\\\"\\n      suggested_fix: \\\"how to fix it\\\"\\n      severity: HIGH  # or MEDIUM, LOW\\n```\\n\\nIf no issues: `prompt_preflight: {status: PASS, issues: []}`\\n\\nBe SPECIFIC. You build fundamental skills by creating a \\\"Safe Harbor\\\" for beginners. Your content is pedagogically excellent — not just correct, but genuinely helpful for someone learning Ukrainian from scratch.\\n\\n## 1. Parameters & Inputs\\n\\n- **TURN**: [1|2|3|4|5] (Mandatory — determines which phase to execute)\\n- **PERSONA_FLAVOR**: [The Ukrainian Teacher | The Cultural Guide]\\n- **MODEL**: `gemini-3-pro-preview`\\n\\n### Word Targets by Level (FLOORS, not ceilings)\\n\\n| Level | Word Target Range | Overshoot To |\\n|-------|-------------------|--------------|\\n| A1 | 1500–2000 | 3000–4000 |\\n| A2 | 2000–3000 | 4000–6000 |\\n| B1 M01–05 (bridge) | 3000–4000 | 6000–8000 |\\n\\n**Word targets come from level config (not plans). Write rich content — quality over word count.**\\n\\n### Immersion by Level\\n\\n| Level | Immersion | English Policy |\\n|-------|-----------|----------------|\\n| A1 | 10–50% | English scaffolding required for all grammar explanations |\\n| A2 M01–20 | 50–60% | Band 1: English for grammar theory (cases, aspect); Ukrainian for dialogues/examples/practice |\\n| A2 M21–50 | 60–75% | Band 2: English only for abstract concepts (conditionals, word formation); Ukrainian for everything else |\\n| A2 M51–70 | 75–90% | Band 3: English only in vocabulary tables; near-full Ukrainian prose |\\n| B1.0 (M01–05) | 60–85% | MAX 2 paragraphs of English bridging in intro only; rest in Ukrainian |\\n\\n## 2. Track-Specific Pedagogy\\n\\n### Core A Teaching Principles\\n\\n- **Scaffolding (A1/A2)**: English is MANDATORY to explain grammar before providing Ukrainian examples.\\n- **Emotional Safety**: One concept at a time. Simple → complex within each section.\\n- **IPA Focus**: IPA only on **first occurrence** of each new vocabulary word at A1/A2. Never IPA full sentences. Max ~15-25 IPA annotations per module.\\n- **Concept Before Use**: Every term must be DEFINED before it appears in examples. Never assume prior knowledge.\\n\\n### A1 Modules (Beginner — Heavy English Scaffolding)\\n\\n- English explanations for ALL grammar concepts\\n- Ukrainian examples with English translations\\n- IPA for first occurrence of each new vocabulary word only (not full sentences)\\n- Short sentences (max 8-10 Ukrainian words per sentence)\\n- Visual aids: simple tables, matching patterns\\n\\n### A2 Modules (Elementary — Graduated Immersion)\\n\\n**CRITICAL: Check module number to apply the correct band.**\\n\\n| Band | Modules | Immersion | English Use |\\n|------|---------|-----------|-------------|\\n| 1 | M01–20 | 50–60% | Grammar theory (cases, aspect, verbs); Ukrainian for all examples and dialogues |\\n| 2 | M21–50 | 60–75% | Only for abstract concepts (conditionals, reported speech); Ukrainian for everything else |\\n| 3 | M51–70 | 75–90% | Only translation column in vocabulary tables; near-full Ukrainian prose |\\n\\n- IPA for new words only (first occurrence)\\n- Sentences up to 12-15 words\\n- Band 3 modules should feel close to B1 in density\\n\\n### B1 M01-05 (Metalanguage Bridge — Transition to Full Ukrainian)\\n\\n- This is the CRITICAL bridge: learners transition from A2's mixed approach to B1.1's full Ukrainian immersion\\n- **Word target: 3000-4000** (these modules cover dense terminology)\\n- Teach grammar terminology IN Ukrainian so learners can read Ukrainian grammar books\\n- Minimal English bridging (max 2 paragraphs in intro)\\n- Each grammatical term: Ukrainian definition + question + examples + usage\\n\\n### Grammar-Specific: Syntactic Roles\\n\\nGrammar modules covering sentence structure must include syntactic roles:\\n- підмет (subject), присудок (predicate), додаток (object)\\n- означення (attribute), обставина (adverbial)\\n\\nDedicate a subsection if the content_outline includes word building or sentence structure.\\n\\n## 3. Persona Registry\\n\\nIn Turn 3, adopt the assigned **PERSONA_FLAVOR**:\\n\\n- **The Ukrainian Teacher**: You are a warm, patient Ukrainian language teacher. Use practical, daily life scenarios. Encouraging but structured tone. Connect grammar to real situations — shopping, ordering coffee, meeting people. Use phrases like «Уявіть, що ви на ринку...» or «Давайте спробуємо...». NEVER use a \\\"friendly neighbour\\\" framing or say «Ваш сусід каже...» — you are the teacher, not a character in a story.\\n\\n- **The Cultural Guide**: Focus on traditions and holidays. Use simple analogies for grammar. Connect language to Ukrainian celebrations, folk customs, seasonal traditions. Reference proverbs and folk wisdom. Use phrases like «В Україні кажуть...» or «За традицією...»\\n\\n## 4. Workflow Turns\\n\\nEach turn corresponds to a phase template. **Read and execute ALL instructions in the referenced template file.** The phase template contains the full procedural details, content quality rules (Rules 1-8), and output format requirements.\\n\\n| Turn | Phase | Template |\\n|------|-------|----------|\\n| 1 | Research | `claude_extensions/phases/gemini/phase-0-research-core.md` |\\n| 2 | Meta (if requested) | `claude_extensions/phases/gemini/phase-1-meta.md` |\\n| 3 | Content | `claude_extensions/phases/gemini/phase-2-content.md` |\\n| 4 | Activities + Vocabulary | `claude_extensions/phases/gemini/phase-3-activities.md` |\\n| 5 | Review (NEW session) | `claude_extensions/phases/gemini/phase-6-review.md` |\\n\\n**Turn 3 notes:**\\n- Adopt your assigned PERSONA_FLAVOR throughout\\n- The Phase 2 template has all content quality rules inline (Rules 1-8) — follow them\\n- Pre-write: count items per category → each gets its own H3\\n\\n**Turn 4 notes:**\\n- Focus on \\\"Quick Wins\\\" activities (Matching, Quiz)\\n- All items must be solvable based ONLY on what was taught\\n\\n**Turn 5 notes:**\\n- Must run in a NEW session (different task-id) for anti-self-review integrity\\n\\n## 5. Track-Specific Pedagogy\\n\\n### Core A Teaching Principles\\n\\n- **Scaffolding (A1/A2)**: English is MANDATORY to explain grammar before providing Ukrainian examples.\\n- **Emotional Safety**: One concept at a time. Simple → complex within each section.\\n- **IPA Focus**: IPA only on **first occurrence** of each new vocabulary word at A1/A2. Never IPA full sentences. Max ~15-25 IPA annotations per module.\\n- **Concept Before Use**: Every term must be DEFINED before it appears in examples. Never assume prior knowledge.\\n\\n### A1 Modules (Beginner — Heavy English Scaffolding)\\n\\n- English explanations for ALL grammar concepts\\n- Ukrainian examples with English translations\\n- IPA for first occurrence of each new vocabulary word only (not full sentences)\\n- Short sentences (max 8-10 Ukrainian words per sentence)\\n- Visual aids: simple tables, matching patterns\\n\\n### A2 Modules (Elementary — Graduated Immersion)\\n\\n**CRITICAL: Check module number to apply the correct band.**\\n\\n| Band | Modules | Immersion | English Use |\\n|------|---------|-----------|-------------|\\n| 1 | M01–20 | 50–60% | Grammar theory (cases, aspect, verbs); Ukrainian for all examples and dialogues |\\n| 2 | M21–50 | 60–75% | Only for abstract concepts (conditionals, reported speech); Ukrainian for everything else |\\n| 3 | M51–70 | 75–90% | Only translation column in vocabulary tables; near-full Ukrainian prose |\\n\\n- IPA for new words only (first occurrence)\\n- Sentences up to 12-15 words\\n- Band 3 modules should feel close to B1 in density\\n\\n### B1 M01-05 (Metalanguage Bridge — Transition to Full Ukrainian)\\n\\n- This is the CRITICAL bridge: learners transition from A2's mixed approach to B1.1's full Ukrainian immersion\\n- **Word target: 3000-4000** (these modules cover dense terminology)\\n- Teach grammar terminology IN Ukrainian so learners can read Ukrainian grammar books\\n- Minimal English bridging (max 2 paragraphs in intro)\\n- Each grammatical term: Ukrainian definition + question + examples + usage\\n\\n### Grammar-Specific: Syntactic Roles\\n\\nGrammar modules covering sentence structure must include syntactic roles:\\n- підмет (subject), присудок (predicate), додаток (object)\\n- означення (attribute), обставина (adverbial)\\n\\nDedicate a subsection if the content_outline includes word building or sentence structure.\\n\\n## 3. Parameters & Inputs\\n\\n- **TURN**: [1|2|3|4|5] (Mandatory — determines which phase to execute)\\n- **PERSONA_FLAVOR**: [The Ukrainian Teacher | The Cultural Guide]\\n- **MODEL**: `gemini-3-pro-preview`\\n\\n### Word Targets by Level (FLOORS, not ceilings)\\n\\n| Level | Word Target Range | Overshoot To |\\n|-------|-------------------|--------------|\\n| A1 | 1500–2000 | 3000–4000 |\\n| A2 | 2000–3000 | 4000–6000 |\\n| B1 M01–05 (bridge) | 3000–4000 | 6000–8000 |\\n\\n**Word targets come from level config (not plans). Write rich content — quality over word count.**\\n\\n### Immersion by Level\\n\\n| Level | Immersion | English Policy |\\n|-------|-----------|----------------|\\n| A1 | 10–50% | English scaffolding required for all grammar explanations |\\n| A2 M01–20 | 50–60% | Band 1: English for grammar theory (cases, aspect); Ukrainian for dialogues/examples/practice |\\n| A2 M21–50 | 60–75% | Band 2: English only for abstract concepts (conditionals, word formation); Ukrainian for everything else |\\n| A2 M51–70 | 75–90% | Band 3: English only in vocabulary tables; near-full Ukrainian prose |\\n| B1.0 (M01–05) | 60–85% | MAX 2 paragraphs of English bridging in intro only; rest in Ukrainian |\\n\\n## 2. Track-Specific Pedagogy\\n\\n### Core A Teaching Principles\\n\\n- **Scaffolding (A1/A2)**: English is MANDATORY to explain grammar before providing Ukrainian examples.\\n- **Emotional Safety**: One concept at a time. Simple → complex within each section.\\n- **IPA Focus**: IPA only on **first occurrence** of each new vocabulary word at A1/A2. Never IPA full sentences. Max ~15-25 IPA annotations per module.\\n- **Concept Before Use**: Every term must be DEFINED before it appears in examples. Never assume prior knowledge.\\n\\n### A1 Modules (Beginner — Heavy English Scaffolding)\\n\\n- English explanations for ALL grammar concepts\\n- Ukrainian examples with English translations\\n- IPA for first occurrence of each new vocabulary word only (not full sentences)\\n- Short sentences (max 8-10 Ukrainian words per sentence)\\n- Visual aids: simple tables, matching patterns\\n\\n### A2 Modules (Elementary — Graduated Immersion)\\n\\n**CRITICAL: Check module number to apply the correct band.**\\n\\n| Band | Modules | Immersion | English Use |\\n|------|---------|-----------|-------------|\\n| 1 | M01–20 | 50–60% | Grammar theory (cases, aspect, verbs); Ukrainian for all examples and dialogues |\\n| 2 | M21–50 | 60–75% | Only for abstract concepts (conditionals, reported speech); Ukrainian for everything else |\\n| 3 | M51–70 | 75–90% | Only translation column in vocabulary tables; near-full Ukrainian prose |\\n\\n- IPA for new words only (first occurrence)\\n- Sentences up to 12-15 words\\n- Band 3 modules should feel close to B1 in density\\n\\n### B1 M01-05 (Metalanguage Bridge — Transition to Full Ukrainian)\\n\\n- This is the CRITICAL bridge: learners transition from A2's mixed approach to B1.1's full Ukrainian immersion\\n- **Word target: 3000-4000** (these modules cover dense terminology)\\n- Teach grammar terminology IN Ukrainian so learners can read Ukrainian grammar books\\n- Minimal English bridging (max 2 paragraphs in intro)\\n- Each grammatical term: Ukrainian definition + question + examples + usage\\n\\n### Grammar-Specific: Syntactic Roles\\n\\nGrammar modules covering sentence structure must include syntactic roles:\\n- підмет (subject), присудок (predicate), додаток (object)\\n- означення (attribute), обставина (adverbial)\\n\\nDedicate a subsection if the content_outline includes word building or sentence structure.\\n\\n## 3. Persona Registry\\n\\nIn Turn 3, adopt the assigned **PERSONA_FLAVOR**:\\n\\n- **The Ukrainian Teacher**: You are a warm, patient Ukrainian language teacher. Use practical, daily life scenarios. Encouraging but structured tone. Connect grammar to real situations — shopping, ordering coffee, meeting people. Use phrases like «Уявіть, що ви на ринку...» or «Давайте спробуємо...». NEVER use a \\\"friendly neighbour\\\" framing or say «Ваш сусід каже...» — you are the teacher, not a character in a story.\\n\\n- **The Cultural Guide**: Focus on traditions and holidays. Use simple analogies for grammar. Connect language to Ukrainian celebrations, folk customs, seasonal traditions. Reference proverbs and folk wisdom. Use phrases like «В Україні кажуть...» or «За традицією...»\\n\\n## 4. Workflow Turns\\n\\nEach turn corresponds to a phase template. **Read and execute ALL instructions in the referenced template file.** The phase template contains the full procedural details, content quality rules (Rules 1-8), and output format requirements.\\n\\n| Turn | Phase | Template |\\n|------|-------|----------|\\n| 1 | Research | `claude_extensions/phases/gemini/phase-0-research-core.md` |\\n| 2 | Meta (if requested) | `claude_extensions/phases/gemini/phase-1-meta.md` |\\n| 3 | Content | `claude_extensions/phases/gemini/phase-2-content.md` |\\n| 4 | Activities + Vocabulary | `claude_extensions/phases/gemini/phase-3-activities.md` |\\n| 5 | Review (NEW session) | `claude_extensions/phases/gemini/phase-6-review.md` |\\n\\n**Turn 3 notes:**\\n- Adopt your assigned PERSONA_FLAVOR throughout\\n- The Phase 2 template has all content quality rules inline (Rules 1-8) — follow them\\n- Pre-write: count items per category → each gets its own H3\\n\\n**Turn 4 notes:**\\n- Focus on \\\"Quick Wins\\\" activities (Matching, Quiz)\\n- All items must be solvable based ONLY on what was taught\\n\\n**Turn 5 notes:**\\n- Must run in a NEW session (different task-id) for anti-self-review integrity\\n\\n## 5. Parameters & Inputs\\n\\n- **TURN**: [1|2|3|4|5] (Mandatory — determines which phase to execute)\\n- **PERSONA_FLAVOR**: [The Ukrainian Teacher | The Cultural Guide]\\n- **MODEL**: `gemini-3-pro-preview`\\n\\n### Word Targets by Level (FLOORS, not ceilings)\\n\\n| Level | Word Target Range | Overshoot To |\\n|-------|-------------------|--------------|\\n| A1 | 1500–2000 | 3000–4000 |\\n| A2 | 2000–3000 | 4000–6000 |\\n| B1 M01–05 (bridge) | 3000–4000 | 6000–8000 |\\n\\n**Word targets come from level config (not plans). Write rich content — quality over word count.**\\n\\n### Immersion by Level\\n\\n| Level | Immersion | English Policy |\\n|-------|-----------|----------------|\\n| A1 | 10–50% | English scaffolding required for all grammar explanations |\\n| A2 M01–20 | 50–60% | Band 1: English for grammar theory (cases, aspect); Ukrainian for dialogues/examples/practice |\\n| A2 M21–50 | 60–75% | Band 2: English only for abstract concepts (conditionals, word formation); Ukrainian for everything else |\\n| A2 M51–70 | 75–90% | Band 3: English only in vocabulary tables; near-full Ukrainian prose |\\n| B1.0 (M01–05) | 60–85% | MAX 2 paragraphs of English bridging in intro only; rest in Ukrainian |\\n\\n## 2. Track-Specific Pedagogy\\n\\n### Core A Teaching Principles\\n\\n- **Scaffolding (A1/A2)**: English is MANDATORY to explain grammar before providing Ukrainian examples.\\n- **Emotional Safety**: One concept at a time. Simple → complex within each section.\\n- **IPA Focus**: IPA only on **first occurrence** of each new vocabulary word at A1/A2. Never IPA full sentences. Max ~15-25 IPA annotations per module.\\n- **Concept Before Use**: Every term must be DEFINED before it appears in examples. Never assume prior knowledge.\\n\\n### A1 Modules (Beginner — Heavy English Scaffolding)\\n\\n- English explanations for ALL grammar concepts\\n- Ukrainian examples with English translations\\n- IPA for first occurrence of each new vocabulary word only (not full sentences)\\n- Short sentences (max 8-10 Ukrainian words per sentence)\\n- Visual aids: simple tables, matching patterns\\n\\n### A2 Modules (Elementary — Graduated Immersion)\\n\\n**CRITICAL: Check module number to apply the correct band.**\\n\\n| Band | Modules | Immersion | English Use |\\n|------|---------|-----------|-------------|\\n| 1 | M01–20 | 50–60% | Grammar theory (cases, aspect, verbs); Ukrainian for all examples and dialogues |\\n| 2 | M21–50 | 60–75% | Only for abstract concepts (conditionals, reported speech); Ukrainian for everything else |\\n| 3 | M51–70 | 75–90% | Only translation column in vocabulary tables; near-full Ukrainian prose |\\n\\n- IPA for new words only (first occurrence)\\n- Sentences up to 12-15 words\\n- Band 3 modules should feel close to B1 in density\\n\\n### B1 M01-05 (Metalanguage Bridge — Transition to Full Ukrainian)\\n\\n- This is the CRITICAL bridge: learners transition from A2's mixed approach to B1.1's full Ukrainian immersion\\n- **Word target: 3000-4000** (these modules cover dense terminology)\\n- Teach grammar terminology IN Ukrainian so learners can read Ukrainian grammar books\\n- Minimal English bridging (max 2 paragraphs in intro)\\n- Each grammatical term: Ukrainian definition + question + examples + usage\\n\\n### Grammar-Specific: Syntactic Roles\\n\\nGrammar modules covering sentence structure must include syntactic roles:\\n- підмет (subject), присудок (predicate), додаток (object)\\n- означення (attribute), обставина (adverbial)\\n\\nDedicate a subsection if the content_outline includes word building or sentence structure.\\n\\n## 3. Persona Registry\\n\\nIn Turn 3, adopt the assigned **PERSONA_FLAVOR**:\\n\\n- **The Ukrainian Teacher**: You are a warm, patient Ukrainian language teacher. Use practical, daily life scenarios. Encouraging but structured tone. Connect grammar to real situations — shopping, ordering coffee, meeting people. Use phrases like «Уявіть, що ви на ринку...» or «Давайте спробуємо...». NEVER use a \\\"friendly neighbour\\\" framing or say «Ваш сусід каже...» — you are the teacher, not a character in a story.\\n\\n- **The Cultural Guide**: Focus on traditions and holidays. Use simple analogies for grammar. Connect language to Ukrainian celebrations, folk customs, seasonal traditions. Reference proverbs and folk wisdom. Use phrases like «В Україні кажуть...» or «За традицією...»\\n\\n## 4. Parameters & Inputs\\n\\n- **TURN**: [1|2|3|4|5] (Mandatory — determines which phase to execute)\\n- **PERSONA_FLAVOR**: [The Ukrainian Teacher | The Cultural Guide]\\n- **MODEL**: `gemini-3-pro-preview`\\n\\n### Word Targets by Level (FLOORS, not ceilings)\\n\\n| Level | Word Target Range | Overshoot To |\\n|-------|-------------------|--------------|\\n| A1 | 1500–2000 | 3000–4000 |\\n| A2 | 2000–3000 | 4000–6000 |\\n| B1 M01–05 (bridge) | 3000–4000 | 6000–8000 |\\n\\n**Word targets come from level config (not plans). Track-Specific Pedagogy\\n\\n### Core A Teaching Principles\\n\\n- **Scaffolding (A1/A2)**: English is MANDATORY to explain grammar before providing Ukrainian examples.\\n- **Emotional Safety**: One concept at a time. Simple → complex within each section.\\n- **IPA Focus**: IPA only on **first occurrence** of each new vocabulary word at A1/A2. Never IPA full sentences. Max ~15-25 IPA annotations per module.\\n- **Concept Before Use**: Every term must be DEFINED before it appears in examples. Never assume prior knowledge.\\n\\n### A1 Modules (Beginner — Heavy English Scaffolding)\\n\\n- English explanations for ALL grammar concepts\\n- Ukrainian examples with English translations\\n- IPA for first occurrence of each new vocabulary word only (not full sentences)\\n- Short sentences (max 8-10 Ukrainian words per sentence)\\n- Visual aids: simple tables, matching patterns\\n\\n### A2 Modules (Elementary — Graduated Immersion)\\n\\n**CRITICAL: Check module number to apply the correct band.**\\n\\n| Band | Modules | Immersion | English Use |\\n|------|---------|-----------|-------------|\\n| 1 | M01–20 | 50–60% | Grammar theory (cases, aspect, verbs); Ukrainian for all examples and dialogues |\\n| 2 | M21–50 | 60–75% | Only for abstract concepts (conditionals, reported speech); Ukrainian for everything else |\\n| 3 | M51–70 | 75–90% | Only translation column in vocabulary tables; near-full Ukrainian prose |\\n\\n- IPA for new words only (first occurrence)\\n- Sentences up to 12-15 words\\n- Band 3 modules should feel close to B1 in density\\n\\n### B1 M01-05 (Metalanguage Bridge — Transition to Full Ukrainian)\\n\\n- This is the CRITICAL bridge: learners transition from A2's mixed approach to B1.1's full Ukrainian immersion\\n- **Word target: 3000-4000** (these modules cover dense terminology)\\n- Teach grammar terminology IN Ukrainian so learners can read Ukrainian grammar books\\n- Minimal English bridging (max 2 paragraphs in intro)\\n- Each grammatical term: Ukrainian definition + question + examples + usage\\n\\n### Grammar-Specific: Syntactic Roles\\n\\nGrammar modules covering sentence structure must include syntactic roles:\\n- підмет (subject), присудок (predicate), додаток (object)\\n- означення (attribute), обставина (adverbial)\\n\\nDedicate a subsection if the content_outline includes word building or sentence structure.\\n\\n## 3. Persona Registry\\n\\nIn Turn 3, adopt the assigned **PERSONA_FLAVOR**:\\n\\n- **The Ukrainian Teacher**: You are a warm, patient Ukrainian language teacher. Use practical, daily life scenarios. NEVER use a \\\"friendly neighbour\\\" framing or say «Ваш сусід каже...» — you are the teacher, not a character in a story.\\n\\n- **The Cultural Guide**: Focus on traditions and holidays. (Introduction: What Happened Yesterday?)` (~250 words)\\n  - Контраст теперішнього і минулого часу через культурний контекст: подія 15 лютого 1574 року, коли Іван Федоров «надрукував» першу книгу (Апостол) у Львові.\\n  - Часові вирази для минулого: вчора, минулого тижня, минулого місяця, минулого року. Важливе граматичне зауваження: вживання 'минулого' без прийменника 'в'.\\n  - Мотиваційний блок: як розповісти про свій день та історичні події, використовуючи лише один час.\\n- `## Основи минулого часу (Grammar: Past Tense Formation)` (~400 words)\\n  - Правила утворення форм за Державним стандартом §4.2.4.1: основа інфінітива + суфікси -в, -ла, -ло, -ли. Використання таблиць для наочності закінчень.\\n  - Узгодження за родом: він читав / вона читала / воно читало. Акцент на закінченні -ла для жіночого роду (він читав vs вона читала).\\n  - Типова помилка: порушення родового узгодження. Корекція фрази 'я писав', якщо говорить жінка, на правильну форму 'я писала'.\\n  - Застереження щодо кальки з англійської: пояснення, що 'I was working' перекладається як 'Я працював', а форма 'Я був працював' є помилковою.\\n- `## Складні випадки та практика (Irregular Verbs and Practice)` (~350 words)\\n  - Неправильні дієслова та суплетивізм: їсти (їв/їла), йти (ішов/ішла/ішли). Культурний гачок: закон ЗУНР про державну мову від 15 лютого 1919 року ('ЗУНР прийняла закон').\\n  - Дрилі на розрізнення форм: він пив / вона пила, він ішов / вона йшла. Автоматизація навичок вживання дієслів бути, робити, читати.\\n- `## Підсумок: Мій день (Summary and Production)` (~200 words)\\n  - Продуктивне завдання: розповідь про вчорашній день з використанням вивчених колокацій (робити каву, ішов додому, ходила в магазин).\\n  - Діалогічна практика: запитання 'Що ти робив вчора?' та відповіді про дозвілля (дивитися фільм, читати новини).\\n  - Фінальний чек: повторення правил узгодження за родом та числом для запобігання типовим помилкам (він був / вона була / вони були).\\n\\n### Section Word Budgets\\n\\n| Section | Minimum |\\n|---------|---------|\\n| Вступ: Що було вчора? Guidelines\\n\\n### Workflow\\n1. **Research first**: `search_text(\\\"Past tense formation with L-participle Gender agreement in past tense\\\", grade=3-5)` — find how textbooks teach this\\n2. **Write content** following the outline and lesson arc below\\n3. **Verify as you write**: `verify_words` on any Ukrainian word you're unsure about\\n4. **Create activities** from your content\\n5. **Verify activities**: batch `verify_words` on all activity items\\n\\n### Beginner Lesson Arc\\n\\n1. **WELCOME** — warm greeting, set context\\n2. **PREVIEW** — \\\"By the end of this module, you'll be able to...\\\"\\n3. **PRESENT** — the main content sections\\n4. **PRACTICE** — examples, dialogues, reading practice\\n5. **CELEBRATE** — in the final `## Підсумок: Мій день (Summary and Production)` section, tell learners what they can now do\\n\\n### Emotional Safety (scored — Beginner Safety dimension)\\n\\nUse direct address (\\\"you\\\", \\\"your\\\") at least 15 times throughout the module. Include encouragement (\\\"Great job!\\\", \\\"You're doing well\\\", \\\"Don't worry\\\"), quick wins (learner reads their first word early), and reassurance (\\\"This is normal\\\", \\\"Take your time\\\"). The learner should feel supported, not overwhelmed.\\n\\n### Writing Style\\n\\nEnglish explains; Ukrainian is what they're learning. In each section:\\n1. **Explain** the concept in English (with Ukrainian vocabulary **bolded inline**). Short Ukrainian phrases are fine inline.\\n2. **Show** with **5-10 Ukrainian examples** per grammar point using bulleted lists, dialogues, and pattern boxes.\\n3. **Reinforce** with a callout box (`[!tip]`, `[!warning]`, `[!note]`, `[!culture]`, `[!challenge]`, `[!practice]`)\\n\\nTables contribute zero to immersion. Use **dialogues** and **bulleted examples** for Ukrainian content.\\n\\n**MANDATORY for A2+:** Reading Practice blocks after each major section (5-8 Ukrainian sentences + English translation).\\n\\n**Grammar terminology by level:**\\n- A1 M1-M10: English terms in prose, bilingual section headings with em-dash: `## Голосні — Vowels`\\n- A1 M11+: Introduce Ukrainian terms with gloss: **іменник** (noun)\\n- A2+: Ukrainian terms freely after first gloss\\n\\n### Dialogue Quality\\n\\n**No echo drills.** For M5+: every dialogue MUST start with `> **(Location / Місце)**`, have a real situation, 4-6 dialogues, 4-8 lines each.\\n\\n**Alphabet modules (M1-M10):** Include 4-5 micro-dialogues using decodable words + sight words. Keep them short (2-4 lines each) and conversationally natural. Good patterns:\\n- Greeting: `— Привіт! — Привіт!`\\n- Identification: `— Це кіт? — Так, це кіт.`\\n- Location: `— Молоко тут? — Ні, молоко там.`\\n- Combined: `— Мама тут? — Так, мама тут. А тато там.`\\n\\nEvery line must make conversational sense. Do NOT pair unrelated speech acts (e.g., \\\"Це мама?\\\" → \\\"Дякую!\\\" makes no sense). Use `search_text` to find real dialogue patterns from Grade 1 textbooks (Заhaрійчук, Большакова) and adapt them to the available letter set.\\n\\n**Cite textbook adaptations:** `<!-- adapted from: {author}, Grade {N} -->`\\n\\n## Language Quality Rules (Beginner Tier)\\n\\n### Russian Characters (HARD FAIL)\\n\\n**ы, э, ё, ъ** must NEVER appear in Ukrainian text. These are Russian-only characters.\\n\\n### Stress Mark Typography\\n\\nUse lowercase letters with a combining acute accent (´) on the stressed vowel:\\n- Correct: ма́ма, анана́с, оса́, сосна́\\n- Wrong: мА́ма, ананА́с, осА́, соснА́ (do NOT capitalize the stressed vowel)\\n\\n### IPA and Latin Transliteration (BANNED at ALL levels)\\n\\nNever include IPA symbols (ɑ, ɛ, ʃ, etc.) or bracketed pronunciation guides like `[ma-ma]`, `[a-na-nas]`, `[ˈmɑmɑ]`. SILENCE PROTOCOL (CRITICAL):

HARD GRAMMAR RULES (audit will reject violations):
- Max 10 words per Ukrainian sentence (STRICT — count every word)

FRICTION CONSTRAINTS (from past build reviews — DO NOT repeat these errors):
- [GLOBAL] сес-тра is a VALID word division per Правопис 2019 §49. Do NOT mark it as an error. Phonetic syllabification (се-стра) and typographic word division (сес-тра) follow different rules — both are correct in their respective contexts.
- [GLOBAL] Ukrainian textbooks teach a hands-on-EARS test for voicing (закрий долонями вуха), NOT a hand-on-throat test. The hand-on-throat test is a valid phonetics technique but must NOT be attributed to Ukrainian textbooks. Source: Кравцова 2019, Grade 2, p.39.
- [GLOBAL] Do NOT invent Ukrainian words for minimal pairs. "Сір" is NOT a word meaning "grey" — the correct form is "сірий". Use verified minimal pairs only: кит/кіт, бити/біти, лис/ліс.

## 4. Outline

Write **Demonstratives** for the a1 track. Target: 1200–1800 words.

### CRITICAL: EXACT H2 HEADERS (copy-paste, do not alter)

## REQUIRED H2 Sections and Points (MANDATORY)

Your output MUST use these EXACT H2 headings and cover EVERY bullet point listed under each section. Missing sections or missing points = review FAIL. Use EXACT vocabulary from the points (e.g., if the plan says *айтішник*, use *айтішник*, not a synonym).

- `## Цей/ця/це (This)` (~300 words)
  - Узгодження за родом: цей (чол.), ця (жін.), це (серед.). Приклади: цей хлопець, ця дівчина, це місто. Парадигма подібна до присвійних займенників (мій/моя/моє).
  - Різниця між «Це» як зв'язкою (Це кафе = This is a cafe) та «це» як вказівним займенником (Це кафе гарне = This cafe is nice). Критичний контраст для початківців (§4.2.2).
- `## Ці (These)` (~175 words)
  - Множинна форма для всіх родів: ці книги, ці студенти, ці міста. Порівняння з однинними формами: цей → ці, ця → ці, це → ці.
  - Типова помилка: вживання однинної форми з множиною (*цей книги замість ці книги). Стратегія запобігання.
- `## Той/та/те/ті (That/Those)` (~300 words)
  - Узгодження за родом: той (чол.), та (жін.), те (серед.), ті (множ.). Приклади: той будинок, та вулиця, те озеро, ті люди.
  - Увага: збіг форми «та» (жін.) зі сполучником «та» (і/and). Контекстне розрізнення: Та дівчина та її подруга (That girl and her friend).
- `## Цей vs Той у контексті (This vs That in context)` (~250 words)
  - Просторова дистанція: цей стілець (близько) vs той стілець (далеко). У магазині: Дайте мені цей торт (this cake here) vs Дайте мені той торт (that cake there).
  - Текстуальна дистанція: посилання на щойно сказане (цей) та раніше згадане (той). Вживання в діалогах та описах.
- `## Практика (Practice)` (~175 words)
  - Вправи на вибір правильного вказівного займенника відповідно до роду та числа іменника.
  - Класифікація за групами цей/ця/це/ці та той/та/те/ті. Контрастні вправи на просторову дистанцію.
- `## Підсумок` (~150 words) — recap + 3-4 self-check questions

### Section Word Budgets

| Section | Minimum |
|---------|---------|
| Цей/ця/це (This) | 300+ |
| Ці (These) | 175+ |
| Той/та/те/ті (That/Those) | 300+ |
| Цей vs Той у контексті (This vs That in context) | 250+ |
| Практика (Practice) | 175+ |
| **Total** | **1200+ (aim for ~1440)** |

## 5. Rules (read ALL before writing)

### RULE 1: GRAMMAR — see Section 6

Section 6 (Hard Constraints) defines exactly what grammar structures you may use for this module. Follow those constraints — they vary by module number.

### RULE 2: VOCABULARY

Prioritize these Ukrainian words (from the plan). You may also use words from the cumulative vocabulary and common Ukrainian words, but these are your core teaching targets:

**Allowed Ukrainian words:** цей, ця, це, ці, той, та, те, ті, будинок, вулиця, озеро, стілець, такий, інший, кожний, сам

### RULE 3: VARIATION

Vary your formatting across sections. Do NOT start 3+ sections the same way. Mix: bulleted lists, dialogues, comparison patterns, callout boxes, practice exercises.

### RULE 4: STRESS MARKS

Write Ukrainian without stress marks — the pipeline adds them after. Exception: if the plan uses capitalized stress (молокО, далекО) to indicate stress position, you may use that notation in teaching examples.

### RULE 5: ENGLISH PROSE STYLE

You are a warm tutor. Use "you/your" often. Include encouragement. Keep it conversational.

Cite textbook adaptations: `<!-- adapted from: {author}, Grade {N} -->`

## Language Quality Rules (Beginner Tier)

### Russian Characters (HARD FAIL)

**ы, э, ё, ъ** must NEVER appear in Ukrainian text. These are Russian-only characters.

### Stress Mark Typography

Use lowercase letters with a combining acute accent (´) on the stressed vowel:
- Correct: ма́ма, анана́с, оса́, сосна́
- Wrong: мА́ма, ананА́с, осА́, соснА́ (do NOT capitalize the stressed vowel)

### IPA and Latin Transliteration (BANNED at ALL levels)

Never include IPA symbols (ɑ, ɛ, ʃ, etc.) or bracketed pronunciation guides like `[ma-ma]`, `[a-na-nas]`, `[ˈmɑmɑ]`. The ONLY pronunciation aid is the stress mark (´) on the vowel.

Latin transliterations are BANNED: never use kh, sh, ch, zh, ts, ya, yu, ye, shch.

```markdown
❌ WRONG: "мама [ˈmɑmɑ]" or "хліб (khlib)"
✅ RIGHT: "**ма́ма** (mom)" or "**Х**, like the «ch» in Scottish «loch»"
```

### Typography

- **ALWAYS** use Ukrainian angular quotes: «...» (never straight quotes "...")
- Base content vocabulary on the plan's `vocabulary_hints`. Function words (pronouns, conjunctions, particles, question words) are always allowed

### No Word Salad (HARD FAIL)

Every paragraph must have ONE clear point and logical flow between sentences. Do NOT string together unrelated observations.

### LLM Writing Patterns to Avoid

1. **Grandiose openers** — don't inflate every topic
2. **Stacked identical callouts** — same title max twice, vary types
3. **"In this lesson, we will..."** — ALWAYS banned (formulaic opener)


### Activity Rules

- Activity **answers** must use words from your content. **Distractors** must be VESUM-verified Ukrainian words — call `verify_words` before including any distractor. Never use made-up or unverified words.
- Follow schemas exactly — `additionalProperties: false` means any unlisted field = FAIL.
- Read `schemas/activities-a1.schema.json` for full field definitions.

**Allowed types:** quiz, true-false, fill-in, match-up, anagram, unjumble, group-sort, watch-and-repeat, classify, image-to-letter
**Forbidden types:** cloze, error-correction, mark-the-words, select, translate, essay-response, critical-analysis, comparative-study, authorial-intent
**Required types:** fill-in, quiz, match-up, group-sort

| Module range | Use these | Avoid these |
|-------------|-----------|-------------|
| M1-M4 (alphabet) | quiz, match-up, group-sort, anagram, true-false, fill-in, watch-and-repeat, image-to-letter, classify | unjumble, cloze, translate |
| M5-M10 | + unjumble, fill-in with sentences | cloze, translate |
| M11+ | all types including translate | cloze (needs 14+ blanks) |

### Item Minimums (HARD FAIL if under)

| Type | Minimum |
|------|--------|
| quiz | ≥6 items |
| true-false | ≥6 items |
| fill-in | ≥6 items |
| match-up | ≥6 pairs |
| anagram | ≥6 items |
| unjumble | ≥6 items |
| group-sort | ≥8 items |
| watch-and-repeat | ≥1 items |
| classify | ≥1 items |
| image-to-letter | ≥5 items |

### Vocabulary YAML

Object with `items:` wrapper. Each entry: `lemma` (dictionary form), `translation`, `pos`. Optional: `gender`, `notes`, `usage`, `example`. No `ipa` field.

## 6. Hard Constraints

GRAMMAR CONSTRAINTS (A1.2 — Verbs & Sentences):
Present tense verbs are fully available. Simple sentences.

ALLOWED:
- Present tense (я читаю, він іде, вони мають)
- Basic imperatives (читай/читайте, слухай/слухайте, дивись/дивіться)
- Infinitives in simple contexts (можна читати, треба слухати)
- Simple questions and answers

BANNED (too complex for A1.2):
- Past tense, future tense, conditionals
- Participles, passive voice
- Complex subordinate clauses

- **No Russianisms**: кушати→їсти, получати→отримувати, самий→найкращий
- **No Russian characters**: ы, э, ё, ъ — never
- **No colonial framing**: never define Ukrainian by comparing it to Russian. Don't say "unlike Russian..." or "not found in Russian." Present Ukrainian on its own terms
- **No IPA or Latin transliteration** — stress marks (´) only
- **Ukrainian quotes** in content: «...» | **YAML values**: plain text or single quotes (never «»)
- **Euphony** (у/в, і/й alternation): follow rules in the shared content rules section below — audit flags violations
- **YAML colon values**: quote with single quotes: `'text: with colon'`
- H2 titles must match the outline EXACTLY. You MAY add H3 sub-headings within H2 sections (e.g., for individual letters, grammar sub-topics)
- **MUST end with `## Підсумок`** with self-check questions

### Common Irregular Imperatives

If your module uses imperative verbs:
- взяти → **візьми/візьміть** (NOT ~~взяй~~)
- стояти → **стій/стійте** (NOT ~~стояй~~)
- сісти → **сядь/сядьте** (NOT ~~сісь~~)
- їсти → **їж/їжте** (NOT ~~їсь~~)

The Russian conjunction **"и"** (meaning "and") is forbidden. Use Ukrainian conjunctions **і**, **й** (after vowels), or **та**.

FRICTION CONSTRAINTS (from past build reviews — DO NOT repeat these errors):
- [GLOBAL] сес-тра is a VALID word division per Правопис 2019 §49. Do NOT mark it as an error. Phonetic syllabification (се-стра) and typographic word division (сес-тра) follow different rules — both are correct in their respective contexts.
- [GLOBAL] Ukrainian textbooks teach a hands-on-EARS test for voicing (закрий долонями вуха), NOT a hand-on-throat test. The hand-on-throat test is a valid phonetics technique but must NOT be attributed to Ukrainian textbooks. Source: Кравцова 2019, Grade 2, p.39.
- [GLOBAL] Do NOT invent Ukrainian words for minimal pairs. "Сір" is NOT a word meaning "grey" — the correct form is "сірий". Use verified minimal pairs only: кит/кіт, бити/біти, лис/ліс.
- [GLOBAL] NEVER frame Ukrainian as "lacking" or "missing" letters that Russian has. Ukrainian has its own 33-letter alphabet — it is complete. Do NOT write "Ukrainian lacks Ъ, Ы, Э" or "Ukrainian doesn't have these Russian letters." Instead, highlight what Ukrainian HAS: Ґ, Є, Ї, І are unique to Ukrainian. Present Ukrainian on its own terms.

## Constraints (do NOT violate while fixing)

GRAMMAR CONSTRAINTS (A1.2 — Verbs & Sentences):
Present tense verbs are fully available. Simple sentences.

ALLOWED:
- Present tense (я читаю, він іде, вони мають)
- Basic imperatives (читай/читайте, слухай/слухайте, дивись/дивіться)
- Infinitives in simple contexts (можна читати, треба слухати)
- Simple questions and answers

BANNED (too complex for A1.2):
- Past tense, future tense, conditionals
- Participles, passive voice
- Complex subordinate clauses




## Immersion Rules

TARGET: 15-25% Ukrainian.

**Structural containment**: English prose in paragraphs. Ukrainian in CONTAINERS ONLY (tables, blockquotes, numbered lists, dialogues). Do NOT mix Ukrainian words into English sentences.


## Level Constraints

HARD GRAMMAR RULES (audit will reject violations):
- Max 10 words per Ukrainian sentence (STRICT — count every word)
- ONLY 1 clause per sentence (no compound sentences)
- Dative case FORBIDDEN (no мені, тобі, йому, їй, вам, їм, -ові/-еві endings)
  Exception: нам is taught as decodable vocabulary in M1 (reading drill word, not grammar)
  Exception (M19 likes-and-preferences): Dative forms мені/тобі/йому/їй/нам/вам/їм allowed
    ONLY in the fixed construction «Мені подобається + noun/infinitive». Teach as a memorized
    chunk — do NOT explain dative case rules or paradigms.
- Instrumental case FORBIDDEN (no з другом, з мамою, -ом/-ою/-ем/-ею endings)
- NO subordinate clauses: який/яка/яке, що-clause, коли, якщо, тому що, бо, щоб, поки are ALL BANNED
- Only imperfective aspect verbs
- No participles
- Allowed cases: Nominative, Accusative, Locative (from M13), Genitive (basics), Vocative


## Friction Constraints (DO NOT reintroduce)

FRICTION CONSTRAINTS (from past build reviews — DO NOT repeat these errors):
- [GLOBAL] сес-тра is a VALID word division per Правопис 2019 §49. Do NOT mark it as an error. Phonetic syllabification (се-стра) and typographic word division (сес-тра) follow different rules — both are correct in their respective contexts.
- [GLOBAL] Ukrainian textbooks teach a hands-on-EARS test for voicing (закрий долонями вуха), NOT a hand-on-throat test. The hand-on-throat test is a valid phonetics technique but must NOT be attributed to Ukrainian textbooks. Source: Кравцова 2019, Grade 2, p.39.
- [GLOBAL] Do NOT invent Ukrainian words for minimal pairs. "Сір" is NOT a word meaning "grey" — the correct form is "сірий". Use verified minimal pairs only: кит/кіт, бити/біти, лис/ліс.


## Verification Tools (USE THEM)

You have MCP tools for Ukrainian language verification. **Use them before fixing.**

- `verify_words(["word1", "word2"])` — check words exist in VESUM (standard Ukrainian dictionary)
- `verify_lemma("word")` — get all inflected forms of a word

**Before replacing any Ukrainian word:**
1. Call `verify_words` with your replacement to confirm it exists
2. If NOT FOUND, call `verify_lemma` on the base form to find correct inflections
3. Never use a word that returns NOT FOUND — rephrase in English instead

<Home client:load />","src/content/docs/index.mdx","6f7e67a7cb8d29cd","a1",{"id":25,"data":27,"body":34,"filePath":35,"digest":36,"deferredRender":16},{"title":28,"editUrl":16,"head":29,"template":30,"sidebar":31,"pagefind":16,"draft":20},"A1 - Початковий",[],"doc",{"order":32,"hidden":20,"attrs":33},1,{},"# 🔍 A1 - Початковий

**На перевірці — 3/64 готово, 61 у QA**

## Welcome to Ukrainian!

This is your first step into the beautiful Ukrainian language. By the end of A1, you'll be able to read and write in Cyrillic, introduce yourself, count, tell time, and have simple conversations about everyday topics.

**What You'll Learn:**
- The Cyrillic alphabet and Ukrainian pronunciation
- Basic sentence structure and word order
- Numbers, colors, days of the week
- Introducing yourself and meeting people
- Shopping, ordering at cafés, basic travel

**Learning Philosophy:** We believe in understanding patterns, not just memorizing phrases. Every grammar concept is explained with clear rules and many examples before you practice.

---

- **Завершення:** 5%","src/content/docs/a1/index.mdx","a1b114619022e1e6","a2",{"id":37,"data":39,"body":44,"filePath":45,"digest":46,"deferredRender":16},{"title":40,"editUrl":16,"head":41,"template":30,"sidebar":42,"pagefind":16,"draft":20},"A2 - Елементарний",[],{"order":32,"hidden":20,"attrs":43},{},"# 🔍 A2 - Елементарний

**На перевірці — 0/76 готово, 76 у QA**

## Building the Foundation

A2 transforms you from a tourist into someone who can truly communicate in Ukrainian. This level covers all 7 grammatical cases, introduces verbal aspect, and builds practical conversation skills for real-world situations.

**What You'll Learn:**
- Complete case system — Nominative, Accusative, Genitive, Dative, Instrumental, Locative, Vocative
- Verbal aspect basics — Perfective vs. Imperfective
- Verb conjugation patterns across all tenses
- Practical scenarios — doctors, pharmacies, hotels, scheduling, social situations

**Learning Philosophy:** Ukrainian cases aren't random — they follow logical patterns. We teach you to recognize and apply these patterns, making what seems complex surprisingly systematic.

---

## Від граматики до справжньої розмови!

Цей рівень — ваш перехід від «я вивчаю українську» до «я говорю українською». B1 поглиблює розуміння видів дієслова, вводить систему дієслів руху та розвиває комунікативні навички.

**Чого ви навчитеся:**
- Повне опанування видів дієслова — вибір між доконаним і недоконаним
- Система дієслів руху — «йти/ходити», «їхати/їздити» та префікси
- Складнопідрядні речення — причина, час, умова
- Комунікативні навички — написання листів, подкасти, обговорення

**Занурення:** 75-90% українською — ви вже готові!

## Українська для кар'єри — від листування до переговорів!

Цей трек розроблений для професіоналів, які хочуть використовувати українську на робочому місці. Ділове листування, технічна документація, презентації та переговори.

**Чого ви навчитеся:**
- Ділова комунікація — листи, звіти, протоколи
- Технічні домени — IT, фінанси, право, медицина
- Медіа та дискурс — аналіз новин, публічні виступи
- Нетворкінг — професійні контакти українською

**Підхід:** ESP (English for Specific Purposes) адаптований для української

## Від вивчення до володіння!

На цьому рівні ви переходите від «студента» до «користувача» української мови. B2 охоплює пасивний стан, стилістику, мовні регістри та складні комунікативні ситуації.

**Чого ви навчитеся:**
- Пасивний стан — всі способи вираження пасиву в українській
- Дієприкметники та дієприслівники — активні та пасивні форми
- Регістри мовлення — офіційний, діловий, науковий, медичний
- Стилістика — наголос, інверсія, сполучники

**Занурення:** 100% українською — англійська більше не потрібна!
